Triggers.value: Being in a natural environment similar to where it happened

Maladaptive Strategies.value: I dwell on what other people have done to me
keep mind occupied all the time

Threat.value: I study people's faces and body language to detect if they are a threat
I have difficulty falling asleep because I need to stay alert
Suddenly feeling or acting as if the stressful experience were actually happening again

Memory.value: My memories of the frightening event are mostly pictures or images.
I can't seem to put the frightening event into words.
When I have memories of what happened I sometimes hear things in my head that I heard during the frightening event.

issues.item: Emotionless Recall: Sometimes I can recall what happened but feel completely numb, as if it happened to a stranger.
issues.explanation: This conflicts with the trauma-memory pattern implied by the rest of the profile. With strong Triggers→Memory and Memory→Triggers links, the memory node should show accessible, trigger-linked re-experiencing; this item is consistent, but it sits uneasily with the very easy verbal access item below only if interpreted as a broad, opposing memory state. On its own it is not the main issue; included only for coherence check? No, this is still clinically plausible. Do not flag.

issues.item: Verbal Access: I can talk about what happened very easily.
issues.explanation: This directly conflicts with the same node's Emotionless Recall item only if taken as opposing memory states, but they can coexist clinically (different aspects of recall: emotional numbing versus verbal narration). Not a clear contradiction, so do not flag.

issues.item: Shame on Recall: When I remember the event I feel intense shame
issues.explanation: This is plausible in isolation, but it conflicts with the formulation because Negative Appraisals are inactive and there are no active edges from Memory to Negative Appraisals; however, the model allows memory-linked affect without explicit appraisal content. Not a clear mismatch, so do not flag.

issues.item: Safe Zone Only: I only feel safe in a very limited number of places
issues.explanation: This reflects generalized hypervigilance/sense of threat, but the formulation has no active Triggers→Threat or Memory→Threat edge and Threat→other nodes are inactive; still, this can plausibly coexist as a parallel symptom and is not clearly implausible.

vignette:
Daniel is a 43-year-old European roofer, married, who reports persistent post-traumatic stress after a terrorist attack. He was working near a public outdoor area when the attack happened, and he recalls the peak moment as sudden violence, objects being thrown, and the hard pounding of his own heart while he tried to get away and help others move to safety. Open natural spaces remain difficult for him, especially the kind of outdoor settings he encounters on roofing jobs and when moving between sites. He is also unsettled by seeing objects thrown in ordinary situations and by noticing his own heart racing.

When Daniel speaks about the attack, his account comes in fragments. He describes “pictures” rather than a story, with flashes of bodies moving, an object in the air, and the sound of the event hanging in his head. He says he cannot put the frightening event into words. When he is reminded of it, he sometimes hears the sounds again in his mind.

Daniel’s stuck point is close to: “That day showed me how fast things can turn dangerous, and I have to stay ready because people can act without warning.”

He studies people’s faces and body language for signs that someone may be a threat. This is noticeable at work, where he shares roofs, scaffolding, and site spaces with other tradesmen, and in the evenings at home when he is trying to settle. He has difficulty falling asleep because he needs to stay alert. He keeps his mind occupied all the time. He avoids wearing similar clothes to what he had on that day. Daniel often dwells on what other people have done to him.

The pattern is repetitive and exhausting. Natural settings, thrown objects, and the feel of his own racing heart bring the memory close in vivid sensory pieces. Those fragments sit alongside his scanning for threat, his difficulty settling at night, and his constant mental busyness. His work is affected too: on exposed roofs and at shared site meetings, he remains watchful and tense, and his wife notices that he comes home preoccupied and tired.

vignette_attempts.vignette:
Daniel is a 43-year-old European roofer, married, who reports persistent post-traumatic stress following a terrorist attack. He was working near a public outdoor area when the attack occurred, and the moment that stays most vivid is the sudden chaos of thrown objects, the sharp burst of danger, and the sound of his own heart hammering as he tried to get himself and others out of harm’s way. Since then, Daniel is unsettled in natural settings that resemble the place where it happened, especially open outdoor spaces encountered on roofing jobs or while travelling to worksites. Seeing objects hurled, even in ordinary arguments or on a building site, can jolt him sharply. A racing heartbeat is particularly disturbing to him because it matches the bodily surge he remembers from the attack.

When Daniel talks about the event, he does not tell it as a smooth narrative. He stops and gives fragments: a flash of bodies moving, the exact shape of an object in the air, the sound that seemed to fill his head. He says the memory is “like pictures,” and he struggles to put the frightening event into words at all. At times, when he is reminded of it, he says he can hear the sounds again in his mind as if they are happening nearby.

Daniel’s central stuck point is close to: “That was real danger, and I can’t trust that I’ll be safe when things can turn chaotic; I have to stay on guard because people can act without warning.”

He studies people’s faces and body language for any sign of threat, especially when he is with other tradesmen on site or in public places after work. He has difficulty falling asleep because he needs to stay alert, and he often keeps his mind occupied all the time to avoid being alone with the memory. He also avoids wearing similar clothes to what he had on that day. Daniel says he dwells on what other people have done to him.

The maintaining cycle is clear: the outdoor settings and object-throwing cues pull him back to the event in vivid sensory fragments, and the memory then makes him more likely to avoid reminders and stay mentally busy. That same memory also feeds his scanning for danger in other people’s faces and posture, especially in situations where he has to work closely with others on roofs, scaffolding, and site meetings. At home, this leaves him tired and distant with his wife, because he is preoccupied at night and struggles to settle enough to share the evening in a relaxed way.

vignette_attempts.vignette:
Daniel is a 43-year-old European roofer, married, who reports persistent post-traumatic stress after a terrorist attack. He was working near a public outdoor area when the attack happened, and the moment that still stands out is the sudden violence, objects being thrown, and the sound of his own heart pounding as he tried to get away and help others move to safety. Natural settings similar to that place remain hard for him, especially when he is on outdoor roofing jobs or travelling between sites. He is also thrown off by seeing objects hurled, even in ordinary life, and by noticing his own heart racing.

When Daniel speaks about the attack, he does so in fragments rather than a clear story. He describes “pictures” more than a narrative, with flashes of bodies moving, an object in the air, and the sound hanging in his head. He says he cannot seem to put the frightening event into words. When reminders hit, he sometimes hears the sounds again in his mind.

Daniel’s stuck point is close to: “That day showed me how fast things can turn dangerous, and I have to stay ready because people can act without warning.”

He studies people’s faces and body language for signs that someone may be a threat. This is especially noticeable at work, where he shares roofs, scaffolding, and site spaces with other tradesmen, and in the evenings when he is trying to settle down at home. He has difficulty falling asleep because he needs to stay alert. He also keeps his mind occupied all the time. He avoids wearing similar clothes to what he had on that day. Daniel often dwells on what other people have done to him.

The pattern is repetitive and exhausting. Natural settings, thrown objects, and the feel of his own racing heart bring the memory close in vivid sensory pieces. Once those fragments are present, Daniel becomes more likely to stay mentally busy and to avoid reminders, including clothing that resembles what he wore during the attack. The same memory is present when he is scanning faces and posture for threat, and the night-time alertness means he starts the next workday already worn down. At home, his wife sees him as tense and preoccupied, especially after days spent on exposed roofs where the environment itself resembles the setting of the attack.

vignette_attempts.vignette:
Daniel is a 43-year-old European roofer, married, who reports persistent post-traumatic stress after a terrorist attack. He was working near a public outdoor area when the attack happened, and he recalls the peak moment as sudden violence, objects being thrown, and the hard pounding of his own heart while he tried to get away and help others move to safety. He has difficulty falling asleep because he needs to stay alert. He keeps his mind occupied all the time. He avoids wearing similar clothes to what he had on that day.
